title: Search-head audit-trail completeness — deleted or rewritten search jobs
exclusions: Does NOT detect modifications to events at the indexer layer (covered by 22.35.2); only tampering with the search-head audit trail describing who ran what.
evidence: Saved search 'search_audit_tamper' (every 15 min) outputs any _audit events whose artifact_id has subsequently disappeared from dispatch/, to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Audit controls must capture search activity on ePHI; detecting deletion of the activity trail is one of several required aspects.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that HIPAA §164.312(b) (Audit controls) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.35.5: Search-head audit-trail completeness — deleted or rewritten search jobs.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.35.5' running on _audit (action=search), /services/search/jobs REST for current artifacts, dispatch directory state via Splunk REST., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI 10.3.2 'protect audit log files from modification' is directly supported by this UC.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that PCI-DSS 10.3.2 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.35.5: Search-head audit-trail completeness — deleted or rewritten search jobs.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.35.5' running on _audit (action=search), /services/search/jobs REST for current artifacts, dispatch directory state via Splunk REST., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: CC7.2 requires anomaly detection over system components; tampering with the activity trail is a specific instance.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SOC-2 CC7.2 (System monitoring for anomalies) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.35.5: Search-head audit-trail completeness — deleted or rewritten search jobs.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.35.5' running on _audit (action=search), /services/search/jobs REST for current artifacts, dispatch directory state via Splunk REST., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: An operator with the correct capability deletes a dispatch job folder; the _audit event survives but the artifact is gone — the UC fires within the next 15-minute interval.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Normal dispatch TTL expiry (configured window) cleans dispatch folders without producing a finding because the _audit event is beyond the retention window.

dataSources: _audit (action=search), /services/search/jobs REST for current artifacts, dispatch directory state via Splunk REST.
spl:
index=_audit action=search info=completed earliest=-24h
| stats values(artifact_id) as artifact_ids earliest(_time) as first_seen latest(_time) as last_seen by search_id user
| mvexpand artifact_ids
| join type=outer artifact_ids [| rest /services/search/jobs splunk_server=* | fields sid dispatchState | rename sid as artifact_ids]
| where isnull(dispatchState) AND (now()-last_seen)<86400
| table user search_id artifact_ids first_seen last_seen
description: Reconciles search-head _audit events against the current dispatch artifact state to detect search jobs that were recorded as completed but whose artifacts have since been removed outside the normal dispatch TTL. Mass deletion of dispatch artefacts is a classic post-incident tampering pattern.
value: Gives the Splunk admin team a defensible 'every search run is accounted for' control rather than the weaker 'we have search logging enabled'.
implementation: (1) Ensure _audit is retained for ≥90 days on the evidence cluster; (2) adjust dispatch_ttl per risk tier; (3) schedule every 15 min; (4) on hit, SOAR opens a P2 for the Splunk admin team. Indexes required: `index=_audit`.
visualization: Time chart of job-vs-artifact reconciliation, table of missing artifacts by user, single-value 'search jobs with artifact present (7d)'.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK — T1070.006 Indicator Removal: Timestomp

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `_audit` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `§164.312(b)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that HIPAA §164.312(b) (Audit controls) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.35.5: Search-head audit-trail completeness — deleted or rewritten search jobs." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `_audit` under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Ensure _audit is retained for ≥90 days on the evidence cluster. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** adjust dispatch_ttl per risk tier. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** schedule every 15 min. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** on hit, SOAR opens a P2 for the Splunk admin team. Automated remediation must be gated behind a defined approval workflow; compliance controls that take destructive action without human review are themselves a governance risk.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Search-head audit-trail completeness — deleted or rewritten search jobs," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the HIPAA control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`_audit`, sourcetype=`the primary`) with a subsearch on `artifact_ids` across the last 24 hours. `type=outer` (left-outer) returns every row from the outer search and fills nulls where the inner search has no match. Use this when the compliance question is 'for every row in A, what does B say?' — the null pattern is itself the finding (e.g. 'ticket opened but no PIR recorded').

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.35.5: Search-head audit-trail completeness — deleted or rewritten ]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.35.5,reg=HIPAA
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=_audit sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `search_id`, `artifact_id`, `user`, `_time`. Run:

```spl
index=_audit sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table search_id, artifact_id, user, _time
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.35.5: HIPAA evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Time Chart Of Job-Vs-Artifact Reconciliation (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Table Of Missing Artifacts By User (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value 'Search Jobs With Artifact Present (7D)' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**HIPAA-specific failure modes:**

• Minimum-necessary principle: access-anomaly detections (excessive chart views, unexpected departments) must NOT flag clinicians accessing records for treatment purposes — §164.502(b) explicitly permits this. Join detections against the clinician's current department and patient's active encounter to avoid generating thousands of false positives on ED doctors seeing admitted patients.

• Break-glass and emergency access (§164.312(a)(2)(ii)): emergency accounts will legitimately bypass normal access controls during downtime events. Maintain a lookup `breakglass_declarations.csv` written by the clinical informatics on-call; correlate every break-glass access against a declared event — un-declared emergency access is the finding.

• Audit log retention (§164.312(b)): HIPAA requires 6 years of audit log retention, longer than most Splunk index default settings. Verify `frozenTimePeriodInSecs >= 189216000` for every index named in an HIPAA UC's search, and that the archive tier (S3, cold storage) is provisioned.

• Covered vs non-covered entity scope: if the search unintentionally pulls data from a non-covered business unit (e.g. a research arm not operating as a HIPAA Covered Entity), findings are noise. Filter using the facility/cost-center lookup that the Privacy Officer maintains, not by index name alone.
grandmaExplanation: We watch for breaks in the audit log stream so you can fix gaps before compliance or security reviews find them first.
